data.name: Home.Tagline
data.value: Keeps your and your organization's credentials safe in one place: passwords and logins live in personal and shared team vaults, strongly encrypted. It also supplies your deployed software with its secrets at runtime — API keys, connection strings and more, per application and environment. You decide who sees what, and every access is logged.

data.name: Home.SignInPrompt
data.value: Sign in to manage your vaults and software secrets.

data.name: Login.RememberMe
data.value: Keep me signed in on this device

data.name: Login.FirstAdminHint
data.value: (the first account becomes the system admin)

data.name: Login.LockedOut
data.value: This account is temporarily locked after too many failed attempts. Try again later.

data.name: Login.Unconfirmed.Resent
data.value: Your account is not confirmed yet. We have sent a new confirmation link — please check your e-mail.

data.name: Register.FirstAdmin
data.value: The first account created becomes the system administrator.

data.name: Page.Personal.Desc
data.value: Your private vaults — encrypted and visible to you alone. Store logins, secure notes and other secrets.

data.name: Page.Team.Desc
data.value: Vaults your organization shares — you decide per person who may read, edit or manage.

data.name: Page.Secrets.Desc
data.value: The values your applications read at runtime — API keys, connection strings and more, per application and environment. Pick the application on the left.

data.name: Page.Tokens.Desc
data.value: Your deployed software signs in with a app token to read the software secrets of the application selected on the left. Each token belongs to exactly ONE environment and returns only that environment's values — the Production token reads the Production values, the Test token the Test values. A leaked Test token can therefore never expose Production secrets.

data.name: Vault.List.DescTeam
data.value: Team vaults belong to the tenant and are shared with people via grants. You see the ones you own or were given access to. Sharing a vault lets a colleague read or edit its items.

data.name: Vault.List.DescPersonal
data.value: Personal vaults are yours alone — nobody else can see them. Use them for your own logins, notes and secrets.

data.name: Vault.ConfirmDelete
data.value: Delete this vault and all its items? This cannot be undone.

data.name: Vault.Import.Desc
data.value: Upload a browser password export (Microsoft Edge / Chrome CSV: name, url, username, password, note). Each row becomes a Login item in this vault.

data.name: Vault.Folders.Desc
data.value: Folders group the items of a vault and can contain subfolders. Deleting a folder keeps its contents — items and subfolders move up to the parent folder.

data.name: Vault.Folders.ConfirmDelete
data.value: Delete this folder? Its items and subfolders move up to the parent folder.

data.name: Vault.Items.Desc
data.value: One item is one stored secret — a website login, a secure note or a single value. Values stay encrypted; click an item to see its details.

data.name: Vault.Share.Desc
data.value: Grant a colleague access to this vault: Read to view items, Write to also add/edit, Manage to also rename/delete and share.

data.name: Vault.Share.NoCandidates
data.value: No other users to share with yet.

data.name: Vault.Msg.NoLogins
data.value: No logins found in the file.

data.name: Secret.Desc
data.value: A secret is a named key (for example ConnectionStrings:Main) that can hold a different encrypted value in each environment. Choose View to see or set its per-environment values, or Delete… to open it and remove it. Every change is saved as a new encrypted version, so earlier values are never silently overwritten.

data.name: Secret.View.Desc
data.value: Set or change the value per environment. Each save creates a new encrypted version.

data.name: Token.New
data.value: New token (copy it now — it will not be shown again):

data.name: Token.Issue.Desc
data.value: Issue one token per application and environment. The server names it “application-environment” automatically (numbered when needed) — or give it your own name, e.g. with a host or purpose suffix. Optional: a note (where it runs, owner, ticket) and an expiry date after which it stops working. Restricting each token to a single environment keeps the damage small if one ever leaks.

data.name: Token.Existing.Desc
data.value: Each token is listed with its prefix (the visible start of the value, to identify it), creation date, expiry and status. Edit changes its name and note; Rotate issues a fresh secret value for the same token and immediately invalidates the old one (use it on a schedule or after a suspected leak); Revoke permanently disables it.

data.name: Token.Empty
data.value: No tokens yet — issue one above.

data.name: Token.Msg.Rotated
data.value: Rotated — the previous token has stopped working.

data.name: Error.Heading
data.value: An error occurred while processing your request.

data.name: NotFound.Body
data.value: Sorry, the content you are looking for does not exist.

data.name: Token.UsageNote
data.value: How reading works: store the token in your deployed app's configuration (an environment variable or secret store) and call this server's read API with the HTTP header “Authorization: Bearer <token>”. GET /keyward/api/v1/secrets returns ALL secrets of the token's environment as JSON (key → value; typically loaded into the app's configuration at startup); GET /keyward/api/v1/secrets/{key} returns a single value. The server derives project AND environment solely from the token — the app sends nothing else. Example: curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer amkw_...' https://keyward.example.com/keyward/api/v1/secrets. If a token leaks: Rotate replaces its value, Revoke disables it — your other tokens keep working.

data.name: Secret.UsageNote
data.value: How it fits together: you store a value here once per environment; your deployed software reads it at runtime with a app token (see the App tokens page). Change a value here and the next read picks it up — no redeploy of your app needed.

data.name: Admin.Users.Desc
data.value: All accounts of this installation: unlock a locked account, disable or re-enable it, and assign the tenant role — members use the tenant, tenant admins manage it. The last tenant admin can never be demoted.

data.name: Reset.Forgot.Desc
data.value: Enter your email and we will send you a link to set a new password.

data.name: Reset.Forgot.Sent
data.value: If that address has an account, a password-reset link has been sent.

data.name: Reset.Set.Invalid
data.value: This reset link is invalid or has expired. Request a new one.

data.name: Register.CheckEmail
data.value: If this e-mail address is new, a confirmation link has been sent. Confirm it, then sign in.

data.name: Confirm.Done
data.value: Your e-mail is confirmed. You can now sign in:

data.name: Confirm.Invalid
data.value: This confirmation link is invalid or has expired.

data.name: Page.Personal.Help
data.value: Your private vaults for personal logins, secure notes and other secrets. They belong to you alone — encrypted so that nobody else, not even an administrator, can read them. Use them the way you would a personal password manager: store a login per website, reveal the password only when you need it, and organize items into folders.

data.name: Page.Team.Help
data.value: Vaults owned by your organization (tenant) and shared with colleagues through access grants. Use them for credentials a team needs in common — shared service accounts, Wi-Fi keys, licence keys — where you decide per person who may read, edit or manage each vault. Sharing is explicit: a colleague sees only the vaults you grant them.

data.name: Page.Secrets.Help
data.value: Software secrets are the machine secrets your applications need at runtime — API keys, connection strings, signing keys and the like — stored encrypted and grouped by environment (Development / Test / Preview / Production). Instead of putting these values in your app's own config files, your deployed software fetches them from here at startup through the read API, using a app token. On this page you create and manage the values; on the App tokens page you issue the tokens your software uses to read them.

data.name: Page.Tokens.Help
data.value: App tokens let your deployed software authenticate to {0} and read this project's software secrets at runtime — so secrets never have to live in the app's own config. Each token is scoped to a single environment, so a leaked Production token still cannot read Development. The token's secret value is shown only once, when you issue it — copy it immediately and store it in your app's configuration.

data.name: Home.Tile.Personal
data.value: Your private logins, secure notes and secrets — encrypted and visible to you alone; not even an administrator can read them. Like a personal password manager: for example a website login, a PIN or a licence key.

data.name: Home.Tile.Team
data.value: Shared vaults for your organization — you decide per person who may read, edit or manage. For credentials a team needs together: for example the Wi-Fi password, a shared service account or licence keys.

data.name: Home.Tile.Secrets
data.value: Values your applications read at runtime, instead of keeping them in config files — maintained per environment (Development to Production): for example an API key, a database connection string or a signing key. Changes take effect without redeploying your app.

data.name: Home.Tile.Tokens
data.value: The key your deployed software uses to sign in to {0} and read this project's software secrets. Each token is valid for exactly one environment and can be rotated or revoked at any time — you store it once in your app's configuration.

data.name: Vault.Manage.Desc
data.value: Rename this vault, or delete it permanently.

data.name: Vault.ConfirmDeleteItem
data.value: Delete this item? This cannot be undone.

data.name: Home.Group.Vaults.Desc
data.value: For you and your team — passwords and secrets that people use.

data.name: Home.Group.Software.Desc
data.value: For your applications — secrets that software reads automatically at runtime.

data.name: Vault.CreateCard.DescPersonal
data.value: A vault is like a labelled folder for entries that belong together — its name tells you at a glance what is inside. Several vaults help you separate areas of life: for example “Private” for your own logins, “Work” for job-related ones, “Family” for accounts you use at home, or “Banking” for the especially sensitive.

data.name: Vault.CreateCard.DescTeam
data.value: The name tells everyone with access what is inside at a glance. Several vaults pay off because each vault is shared separately: one per team or department (“IT”, “Accounting”), one per function or task (“Online orders”, “Contracts”), one per customer or project, or “Infrastructure” for server and Wi-Fi credentials that only administrators should see.

data.name: Admin.Users.ConfirmDelete
data.value: Delete the account “{0}” permanently? Their personal vaults and access are removed. This cannot be undone.

data.name: Vault.SelectHint
data.value: Or pick a vault on the left to see its items.

data.name: Vault.Search.Placeholder
data.value: Search all vaults (name, URL, username, note)…

data.name: Vault.Search.Count
data.value: {0} matching item(s) — the password field is never searched.

data.name: Vault.Share.Boundary
data.value: A vault is both the permission and the encryption boundary: every item is cryptographically bound to its vault, and a share always applies to the whole vault — folders are pure organization, not a security boundary. If a different set of people needs access, use a separate vault (e.g. “IT”, “Accounting”, “Customers”) and share it separately.

data.name: Page.Groups.Desc
data.value: Named sets of members — share a vault once with a group (e.g. “IT”) instead of with each person individually. Tenant admins manage groups; a group's own admins manage its members.

data.name: Groups.Members.Desc
data.value: Who belongs to this group. Everyone here receives whatever the group is granted (e.g. shared vaults).

data.name: Groups.ConfirmDelete
data.value: Delete the group “{0}”? Its memberships and every access granted to it are removed.

data.name: Groups.SelectHint
data.value: Pick a group on the left to see its members.

data.name: Groups.Settings.Desc
data.value: Rename this group, or delete it permanently.

data.name: Vault.Export.Desc
data.value: Downloads this vault's LOGIN items as a browser-compatible CSV (name, url, username, password, note) — re-importable here or in Microsoft Edge / Google Chrome. The file contains the passwords in PLAIN TEXT: store it safely and delete it after use. Other item types (notes, values) are not part of this format.

data.name: Secret.SelectHint
data.value: Pick a credential on the left to see and set its values per environment.

data.name: Secret.ConfirmDelete
data.value: Delete “{0}” in every environment? Software reading this key will no longer receive a value.

data.name: Token.SelectHint
data.value: Pick a token on the left to see its details, rotate or revoke it.

data.name: Token.ConfirmRevoke
data.value: Revoke “{0}”? Software using this token immediately loses access — this cannot be undone.

data.name: Secret.Envs.Desc
data.value: This application's environments — every secret holds one value per environment, and every app token is valid for exactly one. A new environment automatically gets an app token (without a value yet) and is immediately available to all secrets. Deleting an environment deletes its values and its app tokens with it; the last environment cannot be deleted.

data.name: Secret.Envs.Msg.Added
data.value: Environment added — including an app token (without a value yet).

data.name: Secret.Envs.ConfirmDelete
data.value: Delete the environment “{0}”? All values in this environment are deleted and its app tokens are revoked. This cannot be undone.

data.name: Token.ConfirmDelete
data.value: Delete token “{0}” permanently? Software still using it stops reading secrets immediately, and the entry disappears from this list (unlike Revoke, this cannot be undone).

data.name: Token.Msg.Reactivated
data.value: Token reactivated — its existing value works again (the expiry date is unchanged).

data.name: Page.Apps.Desc
data.value: An application represents ONE deployed piece of software and bundles its environments, software secrets and app tokens. That way every token reads only its own application's secrets.

data.name: Page.Apps.Help
data.value: Create one application per deployed piece of software (e.g. “webshop”, “backend”). Each application has its own environments (such as Development/Test/Production), its own software secrets and its own app tokens. This separation is the security boundary: a token always belongs to exactly one application and one environment — it can never read another application's or environment's secrets. Every environment automatically gets an app token WITHOUT a value; you generate the value on the app-tokens page when you actually need it — until then the placeholder cannot authenticate. Deleting an application also removes its environments, secrets and tokens.

data.name: App.Empty
data.value: No applications yet — a tenant admin creates one on the left.

data.name: App.SelectHint
data.value: Select an application on the left.

data.name: App.NoneHint
data.value: There is no application yet. Create one on the Applications page first — it bundles one deployed software's environments, software secrets and app tokens.

data.name: App.Settings.Desc
data.value: Rename the application — or delete it in the danger zone below.

data.name: App.ConfirmDelete
data.value: Delete application “{0}” permanently? Its environments, ALL software secrets (including every version) and ALL app tokens are deleted with it — software still using this application's tokens stops reading secrets immediately.

data.name: App.Msg.Created
data.value: Application created — with the default environments and one app token (without a value yet) per environment.

data.name: Home.Tile.Apps
data.value: One application per deployed piece of software — bundles its environments, secrets and app tokens.

data.name: App.Content.Desc
data.value: Opens the page with this application preselected.

data.name: Token.Msg.Generated
data.value: Token value generated — copy it now; it will not be shown again.

data.name: Page.DefaultEnvs.Desc
data.value: The environment set every NEW application starts with (including one pending app token per environment). Existing applications are not affected — their environments are managed on the Applications page.

data.name: DefaultEnvs.BuiltInHint
data.value: This organization uses the built-in default set. “Customize” copies it into an editable list stored in the database.

data.name: DefaultEnvs.ConfirmDelete
data.value: Remove default environment “{0}”? New applications will no longer get it; existing applications are not affected. (Removing all entries returns to the built-in default set.)

data.name: DefaultEnvs.Msg.Customized
data.value: Default set copied — you can now edit it.

data.name: DefaultEnvs.Msg.BackToBuiltIn
data.value: Entry removed — the built-in default set applies again.

data.name: Secret.Msg.DuplicateKey
data.value: The key “{0}” already exists. Select it in the list on the left and set its values per environment there — nothing was overwritten.

data.name: Profile.Notify.Label
data.value: E-mail me when app tokens are about to expire (30/20/10 days ahead, then daily from 9 days).

data.name: Email.Reset.Body1
data.value: We received a request to set a new password for your {0} account.

data.name: Email.Reset.Body2
data.value: Click the button below to choose a new password. This link is single-use and expires shortly.

data.name: Email.Reset.Footer
data.value: If you did not request this, you can safely ignore this e-mail — your password stays unchanged.

data.name: Email.Confirm.Body1
data.value: Welcome to {0}. Please confirm your e-mail address to activate your account.

data.name: Email.Confirm.Body2
data.value: Click the button below to complete your registration.

data.name: Email.Confirm.Footer
data.value: If you did not create a {0} account, you can safely ignore this e-mail.

data.name: Email.TokenExpiry.Intro
data.value: The following app tokens are approaching their expiry date. Rotate them (or issue replacements) before then, or the software using them stops reading its secrets:

data.name: Email.TokenExpiry.Outro
data.value: Rotate a token on the app-tokens page; the new value is shown exactly once.

data.name: Email.TokenExpiry.Footer
data.value: You receive this because you enabled app-token expiry notifications in your profile.

data.name: Page.BreakGlass.Desc
data.value: Dual-control emergency access to team vaults: one system admin requests with a reason, a second approves, and consuming the approved request grants the requester Manage access to the vault - fully audited and revocable.

data.name: BreakGlass.AdminOnly
data.value: Emergency access is restricted to system administrators.

data.name: BreakGlass.Request.Desc
data.value: Request emergency access to a team vault you have no grant on. A different system admin must approve the request before it can be used.

data.name: BreakGlass.Reason.Placeholder
data.value: Reason (required, recorded in the audit trail)

data.name: BreakGlass.OwnRequest
data.value: Own request - a second admin must approve

data.name: BreakGlass.Consume.Hint
data.value: Grants the requester Manage access to the target vault (visible and revocable in the vault's sharing list).

data.name: BreakGlass.Msg.Requested
data.value: Request created - a second admin must approve it.

data.name: BreakGlass.Msg.Approved
data.value: Request approved - the requester can now consume it.

data.name: BreakGlass.Msg.Consumed
data.value: Access granted - the requester now has Manage access to the vault.

data.name: Vault.Items.SelectHint
data.value: Select an entry on the left - or create a new one.

data.name: Token.NoEnvironments
data.value: This application has no environments yet.
